18.title: feat: to markdown api should support Facebook posts
conversationHistory:
AI: > *This response was generated by AI and may contain inaccuracies. Please verify before acting on it.* ## Analysis: Facebook Posts Support for Convert-to-Markdown API ### Current Architecture The project already has a well-established pattern for handling social media content via the Twitter/X implementation: 1. **`get-twitter-content.ts`** - Specialized URL detection + multi-API fallback chain (FxTwitter → VxTwitter → TwitterAPI45 → Twitter135) 2. **`get-html-with-fallbacks.ts:224-236`** - Early interception: checks `isTwitterUrl()` before generic scrape methods 3. **`convert-crud.ts`** - Passes URL to `analyzeUrl()` which calls `getHtmlWithFallbacks()`, so Twitter/Facebook handling is transparent to the convert pipeline ### Proposed Approach Follow the exact same pattern as Twitter: 1. **Create `src/lib/scrape/get-facebook-content.ts`** - Mirror `get-twitter-content.ts` structure: - `isFacebookUrl(url)` - Detect Facebook URLs (facebook.com, fb.com, m.facebook.com, fb.watch) - `extractFacebookPostUrl(url)` - Normalize share URLs to post URLs - `getFacebookContent(url, options)` - Fetch via RapidAPI `facebook-scraper3` - `getFacebookHtml(url, options)` - Return structured HTML (same as `getTwitterHtml`) - `contentToHtml()` - Convert API response to semantic HTML with post text, author, images, reactions, etc. 2. **Update `get-html-with-fallbacks.ts`** - Add `isFacebookUrl()` check alongside Twitter check (line ~224) 3. **Reuse existing `RAPID_API_KEY`** - Already configured in `env.ts:46`. The `facebook-scraper3` API uses the same RapidAPI key infrastructure. ### Implementation Details **Facebook URL patterns to support:** - `facebook.com/{user}/posts/{id}` - `facebook.com/share/p/{id}` - `facebook.com/photo.php?fbid={id}` - `facebook.com/watch?v={id}` - `fb.com/{shortlink}` - `m.facebook.com/...` **API mapping (from issue):** ``` GET https://facebook-scraper3.p.rapidapi.com/post params: { post_url: } headers: { x-rapidapi-key, x-rapidapi-host } ``` **HTML output structure** (semantic, matching Twitter pattern): ```html
{name}

{message}

{images/videos}
{timestamp}
{reactions} reactions, {comments} comments, {shares} shares
``` ### Key Considerations | Aspect | Detail | |--------|--------| | **Cost** | RapidAPI `facebook-scraper3` is a paid API. No known free alternatives for Facebook (unlike Twitter's FxTwitter/VxTwitter) | | **Fallback** | If API fails, fall through to generic scrape methods (same as Twitter pattern) | | **Rate limits** | RapidAPI plan dependent. Shared `RAPID_API_KEY` with TwitterAPI45, Scrappey | | **Privacy** | Facebook posts can be private/friends-only. API may return errors for non-public posts | | **Share URLs** | `facebook.com/share/p/{id}` are redirect URLs - API seems to handle them directly | | **File count** | 1 new file + 2 modified files (minimal footprint) | ### Risk Assessment - **Low risk**: Follows proven Twitter pattern, isolated module, no breaking changes - **Medium concern**: Single API provider (no free fallbacks like Twitter has). If `facebook-scraper3` goes down, only generic scraping remains - **No DB changes**: Pure logic addition ### Suggestions / Questions 1. **Comments support?** The API response includes `comments_id` - should we also fetch comments (like Twitter replies)? This would require a second API call. 2. **Facebook video URLs?** Should `fb.watch` video URLs also be handled? The same API may support them or may need a different endpoint. 3. **Do we need a separate env var?** The RapidAPI host for Facebook (`facebook-scraper3.p.rapidapi.com`) is different from Twitter. Should we add a feature flag like `FACEBOOK_SCRAPER_ENABLED` or just rely on `RAPID_API_KEY` existence? 4. **Album photos** - The API returns `album_preview` with multiple photos. Should all be included in the HTML or just the first? --- *Powered by [ClaudeKit](https://claudekit.cc)*

User: 1. No comments support for now
2. Facebook video URLs -> yes
3. Just rely on RAPID_API_KEY existence
4. All should be included in the HTML

AI: ## Updated Analysis: Facebook Posts Support

All 4 open questions resolved by issue author:

### Confirmed Scope
- **Comments**: Not included (future enhancement)
- **Video URLs**: `fb.watch` supported alongside standard Facebook URLs
- **Config**: No new env var — rely on existing `RAPID_API_KEY`
- **Album photos**: Include ALL photos from `album_preview` in HTML output

### Implementation Summary

**Files:**
- **New**: `src/lib/scrape/get-facebook-content.ts` — URL detection, API fetch, HTML conversion
- **Modify**: `src/lib/scrape/get-html-with-fallbacks.ts` — Add Facebook early interception
- **Modify**: `src/lib/scrape/index.ts` — Export new module (if barrel exports exist)

**URL patterns:**
- `facebook.com/{user}/posts/{id}`
- `facebook.com/share/p/{id}`
- `facebook.com/photo.php?fbid={id}`
- `facebook.com/watch?v={id}`
- `fb.com/{shortlink}`
- `m.facebook.com/...`
- `fb.watch/{id}` ← confirmed in scope

**API**: `GET https://facebook-scraper3.p.rapidapi.com/post` with `post_url` param, using existing `RAPID_API_KEY`

**Fallback**: If API fails → fall through to generic scrape methods (same as Twitter pattern)

**HTML output**: Full `<article>` with author info, post text, ALL album photos, video embeds, reactions/comments/shares stats, timestamp

### Risk: Unchanged
- Low risk, follows proven Twitter pattern
- Single API provider (no free fallbacks)
- No DB changes, no breaking changes

20.title: refactor: website redesign
conversationHistory:
AI: ## Analysis: Issue #20 - Website Redesign

### Current State

ReviewWeb.site uses **EJS templates + TailwindCSS** with a light-first design (white backgrounds, blue accents, standard card layouts). The site has **12 page templates** and **7 common components** (header, footer, mobile-menu, head, etc.).

**Pages to redesign:**
- `home.ejs` - Landing page with review/scan forms
- `pricing.ejs` - Subscription tiers
- `login.ejs` - Auth page (OAuth buttons)
- `profile.ejs` - User dashboard
- `review.ejs` - Analysis results
- `scan.ejs` - Broken link scanner
- `404.ejs`, `privacy.ejs`, `payment-success.ejs`, `checkout-*.ejs`, `workspace-select.ejs`

**Common components:** header, footer, mobile-menu, head, drawers, buttons, inputs

**Master layouts:** `master.ejs` (public pages), `master-dashboard.ejs` (authenticated pages), `master-template.ejs` (share templates)

### Design Direction Requested
- **Dark theme** as primary (not just dark mode toggle)
- **Bold & eye-catching** - strong visual hierarchy, accent colors
- **Trustworthy & professional** - clean typography, trust signals
- **Same structure & features** - pure visual redesign, no functional changes

### Technical Considerations

1. **TailwindCSS already in use** - Can leverage existing utility classes, just swap color scheme and spacing
2. **EJS templating** - No component framework, changes are direct HTML/CSS edits
3. **Dark-first approach** - Current code has `dark:` variants everywhere; redesign should flip to dark-primary
4. **Dependencies** - RemixIcon, Chart.js, Slick carousel, GSAP, Tippy.js all stay
5. **CSS file** - `/css/style.css` needs review for custom styles that may conflict

### Approach Options

**Option A: In-place Tailwind class swap** (Recommended)
- Replace Tailwind classes directly in EJS files
- Dark background as default, remove light/dark toggle complexity
- Fastest approach, lowest risk
- Pros: Simple, no new dependencies, minimal diff
- Cons: Can't A/B test old vs new

**Option B: New CSS theme layer**
- Create CSS custom properties for the new theme
- Keep existing classes, override via CSS variables
- Pros: Easy to revert, supports theming
- Cons: More complexity, potential specificity issues

**Option C: Full template rewrite**
- Rewrite all EJS templates from scratch with new design
- Pros: Cleanest result, no legacy baggage
- Cons: High risk, time-consuming, easy to break functionality

### Recommendation

**Option A** is the way to go. The codebase already uses TailwindCSS extensively. A class-by-class swap to dark-first with bold accent colors is the most efficient and safest approach.

The `ui-ux-pro-max` tools (UUPM) can provide:
- Color palette for dark/bold SaaS theme
- Typography recommendations
- Layout patterns for landing pages
- Component styling guidelines

### Scope Estimate
- **12 page templates** + **7 common components** + **2 master layouts** = ~21 files
- Custom CSS file updates
- No JS logic changes needed
- No backend changes needed

---

### Questions

1. **Color preference**: Any specific accent color in mind? (e.g., electric blue, emerald green, purple/violet) Or should we let the design system recommend?
2. **Dark mode toggle**: Should we keep the light/dark toggle, or go full dark-only?
3. **Existing brand assets**: Is there a logo/brand mark to incorporate, or keep the current text+icon logo?
4. **Reference sites**: Any websites whose design aesthetic you'd like to reference?
5. **Dashboard pages**: The `master-dashboard.ejs` layout (profile, etc.) - same dark treatment or different style for authenticated views?

User: 1. Color preference: electric blue
2. Dark mode toggle: keep
3. Existing brand assets: keep the current text+icon logo
4. Reference sites: nope
5. Dashboard pages: same dark treatment

AI: ## Updated Analysis: Issue #20 - Website Redesign

### Design Direction (Confirmed)

- **Primary theme**: Dark-first with electric blue accent (`#3B82F6` / Tailwind `blue-500` range, possibly brighter like `#2563EB` or `#0EA5E9`)
- **Dark mode toggle**: Keep existing toggle, but default to dark
- **Logo**: Keep current text+icon logo as-is
- **Dashboard pages**: Same dark treatment across all pages (public + authenticated)
- **No reference sites**: Free to design based on best practices for dark SaaS themes

### Color System Plan

| Role | Light Mode | Dark Mode (Primary) |
|------|-----------|--------------------|
| Background | `white` / `gray-50` | `gray-900` / `gray-950` |
| Surface/Cards | `white` / `gray-100` | `gray-800` / `gray-850` |
| Text Primary | `gray-900` | `white` / `gray-100` |
| Text Secondary | `gray-600` | `gray-400` |
| Accent | `blue-600` | `blue-500` / `blue-400` |
| Accent Hover | `blue-700` | `blue-400` |
| Border | `gray-200` | `gray-700` |
| Success | `green-600` | `green-400` |
| Error | `red-600` | `red-400` |

### Implementation Approach: Option A (In-place Tailwind swap)

**Phase 1 - Foundation** (Common components + layouts)
- `master.ejs` + `master-dashboard.ejs` + `master-template.ejs` → dark backgrounds, updated text colors
- `header.ejs` + `mobile-menu.ejs` → dark nav with electric blue accents
- `footer.ejs` → dark footer
- `style.css` → update custom properties, ensure dark-first defaults

**Phase 2 - Core Pages**
- `home.ejs` → bold hero with electric blue CTAs, dark cards
- `pricing.ejs` → dark pricing cards with blue highlights
- `login.ejs` → dark auth page with accent buttons
- `review.ejs` + `scan.ejs` → dark result cards, blue progress indicators

**Phase 3 - Secondary Pages**
- `profile.ejs` → dark dashboard
- `404.ejs`, `privacy.ejs` → dark treatment
- `checkout-*.ejs`, `payment-success.ejs`, `workspace-select.ejs` → consistent dark styling

**Phase 4 - Polish**
- Verify dark/light toggle works correctly in both directions
- Check Chart.js theming (axis labels, grid lines)
- Verify third-party component styling (Slick carousel, Tippy tooltips)
- Responsive testing across breakpoints

### Scope
- **~21 files** to modify (12 pages + 7 components + 2 master layouts + CSS)
- **No JS logic changes** - pure visual/class updates
- **No backend changes**
- **Dark mode toggle preserved** - just flip default to dark-first

### Risk Assessment
- **Low risk**: Class swaps only, no structural changes
- **Toggle compatibility**: Must ensure light mode still looks good (secondary priority)
- **Third-party widgets**: Chart.js, Slick, Tippy may need color overrides for dark bg
- **Accessibility**: Ensure sufficient contrast ratios on dark backgrounds
